hero-subtitle: Product Designer · 7 Years in Tech · B2B SaaS
hero-description: UX/UI designer with front-end development background. I design digital products for B2B and B2C markets, combining design thinking with technical understanding. Growing into Design Engineering—learning to build design systems and implement designs in code.
yoshop-case-desc: POS and inventory system — retail stores, restaurants, 3 platforms
ronto-case-desc: IT company — AI, blockchain, and AR
footer-title: Always open to interesting projects, collaboration, and friendly conversation.
personal-info-desc-1: I've been designing digital products for the past three years, but my journey started earlier — I spent four years as a front-end developer building fintech and SaaS products. That background shapes how I work today: I don't just design interfaces, I understand how they'll be built.
personal-info-desc-2: This means I can have real conversations with developers, make decisions that actually work technically, and create designs that don't fall apart during implementation. I hate when beautiful mockups turn into mediocre products because "it's too hard to build" — so I design with feasibility in mind from the start.
personal-info-desc-3: Right now I'm growing towards Design Engineering. I want to be the person who not only designs the interface but can also implement it in code. I'm learning to build design systems, work with tokens, and bridge that gap between Figma and production.
personal-info-desc-4: What I'm good at: working with cross-functional teams, understanding technical constraints, and creating systems that scale. What I enjoy: solving complex UX problems, building component libraries, and occasionally coding my own projects.
download-cv: Click the image below to download my CV
hobby-description: I love playing volleyball, traveling, and taking photos — new places and people always inspire me. At home, I switch to other creative hobbies like making jewelry, doing crafts, and sometimes drawing.
vixco-case-desc: E-commerce mobile app — buy and sell clothing, cosmetics, and home goods
vixco-hero-subtitle: E-commerce mobile app with dual roles — buy and sell clothing, cosmetics, and home goods in one app
vixco-result-type: Mobile marketplace — clothing, cosmetics, home goods
vixco-result-role: UX/UI Designer — user flows, competitor analysis, full interface design
vixco-result-platform: Mobile app built on Flutter (iOS & Android)
vixco-challenge-text: Design a mobile marketplace where the same user can switch between buyer and seller roles within a single app. The seller side needed product creation flows and a basic sales analytics dashboard — while the buyer side required a smooth, familiar shopping experience.
vixco-challenge-dual: The core design challenge was keeping both roles intuitive without making the app feel bloated. Switching context between buyer and seller needed to feel natural — not like using two different apps.
vixco-solution-roles: Designed a clear role-switching mechanism that keeps the buyer and seller experiences separate but easily accessible. Each role has its own navigation context — the seller's dashboard, product management, and analytics are distinct from the shopping flow.
vixco-solution-seller: The seller interface focused on simplicity: product creation with category selection (clothing, cosmetics, home goods), photo upload, pricing, and a lightweight analytics view showing sales summaries. Designed specifically for non-technical sellers who manage their store on mobile.
vixco-solution-research: Competitor research covered platforms like OLX and Airbnb — both strong examples of dual-role marketplaces — to identify best practices for role switching, listing flows, and trust signals.
vixco-process-text: Worked in a small team of 3 — designer, PM, and Flutter developer. Started with requirements gathering and competitor analysis across 5 platforms. Built out user flows for both roles before moving to wireframes and high-fidelity screens. Regular syncs with the developer ensured designs were Flutter-compatible. Iterated on key flows based on team feedback and stakeholder reviews.
chocofood-case-desc: UX research for a food delivery app — interviews, usability tests, CJM
chocofood-hero-subtitle: UX research for a food delivery app — in-depth interviews, usability testing, and CJM to improve the user experience
chocofood-result-type: UX Research — interviews, usability tests, CJM
chocofood-result-role: UX Researcher — research planning, conducting interviews and usability tests, analysis
chocofood-challenge-text: ChocoFood needed to understand how real users interact with their food delivery app — what causes friction, where they drop off, and what motivates their choices. The goal was to uncover actionable insights that could meaningfully improve the user experience.
chocofood-challenge-scope: The research had to cover both motivations and behavior — why users choose certain restaurants or dishes, and how they actually navigate the app during ordering. This required combining qualitative depth (interviews) with observational data (usability tests).
chocofood-process-briefing: Started with a briefing from the client to align on research goals and key questions. Designed the research structure — prepared interview guides and usability test scenarios based on the brief.
chocofood-process-research: Conducted 10 in-depth interviews to explore user motivations, preferences, and pain points. Then ran 10 usability tests to observe real interactions with the app and identify friction points in the ordering flow.
chocofood-process-analysis: After data collection: transcribed and analyzed interviews, built hypotheses, performed affinity mapping and clustering. Transferred findings into a Customer Journey Map to visualize the full user experience and highlight key moments of friction and opportunity.
chocofood-process-outcome: Delivered a final presentation to the client with all findings and UX improvement recommendations. The client noted that the research could have had significant business impact if implemented.
unitradex-case-desc: B2B distribution platform — web app for distributors and mobile app for stores
unitradex-hero-subtitle: B2B distribution platform connecting distributors and retail stores — full cycle from order to delivery
unitradex-result-modules: 10+ product modules working as a unified system
unitradex-result-platforms: Web platform for distributors + mobile app for stores (iOS/Android)
unitradex-result-role: Lead UX/UI Designer — end-to-end ownership from research to implementation
unitradex-team: Team of 10: front-end and back-end developers, business analyst, project manager. Mentored a junior designer for 6 months.
unitradex-challenge-text: Distributors and stores needed a platform to manage the full order cycle — from catalog to delivery. The process was fragmented and required many manual operations.
unitradex-challenge-complexity: Multiple user roles (warehouse managers, sales teams, store owners), different devices (desktop for office, mobile for stores), complex business logic (pricing, logistics, partner programs) — all needed to work as one system.
unitradex-solution-ownership: As Design Owner I managed all design decisions across 10+ modules and designed user flows for complex business processes across web and mobile.
unitradex-solution-flow: Key solution: a unified order creation flow that simplified the process from product selection to confirmation — accounting for wholesale volumes, special pricing and delivery conditions.
unitradex-solution-ds: Built a scalable design system that ensured consistency across all touchpoints and accelerated development of new features.
unitradex-process-text: Worked closely with front-end and back-end developers, business analyst and project manager. Conducted competitor benchmarking, gathered feedback from test users, and iterated based on results. Defended design decisions in team meetings and controlled implementation quality.
yoshop-metric-platforms-label: Platforms — Monoblock, K9 Android, POS terminal
yoshop-metric-modules-label: Modules — orders, payments, reports, shifts, warehouse, catalog, reservations, admin

geon-metric-platforms-label: Released on App Store & Google Play
creatego-hero-subtitle: No-code builder that empowers designers to build production-ready apps through visual interface
creatego-challenge-text: Designers had to hand off every prototype to developers and wait for implementation — slowing down the entire product cycle. The goal was to let designers build working apps themselves, without writing code.
creatego-challenge-gap: The product required solving a complex design problem: creating a builder interface where each UI element has its own set of configurable properties and behaviors — giving designers real control without overwhelming them.
creatego-solution-bridge: I acted as a bridge between the design and development teams, thanks to my frontend background. This let me translate technical constraints into intuitive design decisions — and design decisions into developer-friendly specs.
creatego-solution-builder: Designed a visual drag-and-drop builder where every element (buttons, divs, inputs, containers) has a configurable properties panel — similar to FlutterFlow. Designers pick components, set properties, and assemble working interfaces without code.
creatego-solution-ds: UI elements are based on the Xela design system — a high-contrast white and blue palette built for clarity and speed.
creatego-process-text: Worked in a startup team over 6 months. The hardest part was designing the builder itself — figuring out how the visual editor should work, how properties should be structured, and how to make it intuitive for designers with no dev experience. Iterated through multiple prototyping rounds before landing on the right interaction model.
geon-case-desc: Mobile streaming platform — live streams, AR, wallet, tournaments
geon-hero-subtitle: Mobile streaming platform — live streams, donations, AR integration, and creator economy tools
geon-result-role: UX/UI Designer — full app design from research to final screens
geon-result-features: Live streams, donations, wallet, AR overlays, tournaments, streamer rankings
geon-challenge-text: Design a mobile streaming ecosystem from scratch — covering live stream creation, viewer interactions, monetization, and creator tools — all in a single coherent mobile experience.
geon-challenge-roles: Three distinct user roles (viewer, agent, advertiser) needed different workflows while sharing the same app. Complex features like AR stream overlays, wallet transactions, tournament brackets, and streamer ranking systems had to feel intuitive on mobile.
geon-solution-flows: Designed the full mobile app experience across all user roles — with clear navigation patterns that adapted to each role's context. Viewers watch, donate, and join tournaments; streamers go live, manage donations, track rankings; advertisers set up and manage campaigns.
geon-solution-ar: Designed the AR integration layer for live streams — interactive overlays that kept the viewing experience immersive without cluttering the stream. Also designed the wallet and donation flows for fast, low-friction transactions.
geon-solution-logo: Designed the brand identity and logo for G-Eon alongside the product.
geon-process-text: Worked with a team of 5 developers and 1 project manager. Covered the full design lifecycle: user research, flow mapping, wireframes, UI design, and prototyping. Regular syncs with developers to validate technical feasibility. Iterated on complex features like the AR overlay system and tournament UI based on team feedback.
ronto-hero-subtitle: Corporate website for an IT company launching startups in AI, blockchain, and augmented reality
ronto-problem: The company needed a unified website to present and promote products across AI, data analytics, blockchain, and AR
ronto-solution: Minimalist, clean landing page showcasing key solutions and services, plus 3 individual product websites
ronto-skills: UI/UX Design, Branding, Logo Design, Mobile Adaptation
ronto-case-about-description: Ronto Tech is an IT company that launches startups in the fields of Blockchain, Data Analytics, AR, and AI. This website showcases their main cases and information about the company.
ronto-logo-description: The meaning of the Ronto Tech logo: the use of the number 10 raised to the power of -27 (Ronto) emphasizes the scale and depth of technological solutions. The first letters illustrate both exponentiation and the essence of the word itself.
ronto-also-made: I also designed 3 product websites for this company:
yoshop-hero-subtitle: Multi-platform POS and inventory system for restaurants and retail stores
yoshop-result-platforms: 3 platforms: Desktop monoblock, Android POS terminal, Tablet (restaurant)
yoshop-result-role: UX/UI Designer - warehouse module, product creation, catalog design
yoshop-result-team-value: 3 designers · 1 product owner · 5-6 developers · 2 QA testers
yoshop-result-company: Finenex (Korea) - product built for the Kazakhstan market
yoshop-team: 3 designers, 1 product owner, 5-6 developers, 2 QA testers. Remote collaboration with Korea-based team.
yoshop-challenge-text: The core challenge was designing a consistent experience across 3 different form factors - monoblocks, Android POS terminals, and tablets - while sharing the same module logic: warehouse, shifts, sales, and reports. Each device had its own constraints and user scenarios that required platform-specific adaptations.
yoshop-challenge-clothing: A major pain point was the warehouse module for clothing stores: staff had to scroll through a long, unfiltered list to find stock by size and model. With hundreds of SKUs and multiple size variants per item, daily inventory work was extremely slow and error-prone.
yoshop-solution-warehouse: Redesigned the warehouse balance view from a flat list into a cross-filter table - staff can now browse stock by any combination of parameters: category, size, color, supplier. Finding and verifying SKU balances became fast and intuitive.
yoshop-solution-templates: Designed a 3-type product creation system: simple product, composite bundle (for promotions with multiple items), and template. The template feature lets staff save any product configuration and reuse it - significantly speeding up catalog management for stores with recurring seasonal assortments.
yoshop-process-text: Worked remotely with Finenex (Korea) as 1 of 3 designers, alongside a product owner, 5-6 developers, and 2 QA testers. Used Figma for all design work, Google Docs and Sheets for specs and documentation. Conducted manual testing on real devices - POS terminals, tablets, and monoblocks - to validate interaction patterns and catch edge cases. Researched American and global analogues to benchmark best practices.
kiosk-hero-subtitle: Inventory and sales management system for retail stores - desktop and monoblock interface
kiosk-result-modules: 4 modules: Warehouse, Sales, Shift management, Reports
kiosk-result-role: UX/UI Designer - warehouse module, product creation, catalog design
kiosk-result-team-value: 3 designers · 1 product owner · 5-6 developers · 2 QA testers
kiosk-result-company: Finenex (Korea) - product built for the Kazakhstan market
kiosk-team: 3 designers, 1 product owner, 5-6 developers, 2 QA testers. Remote collaboration with Korea-based team.
kiosk-challenge-text: The existing warehouse module displayed stock as a long, flat, unfiltered list. For clothing stores managing hundreds of SKUs - each with multiple sizes, colors, and models - finding specific inventory data required scrolling through everything manually, making daily stock checks slow and error-prone.
kiosk-challenge-products: Product creation was also fragmented: there was no way to reuse a configuration when adding similar items. For stores with seasonal collections or recurring product types, this meant rebuilding the same data from scratch every time.
kiosk-solution-warehouse: Redesigned the warehouse balance view as a cross-filter table. Staff can now slice inventory by any parameter combination - category, size, color, supplier - and instantly see grouped totals. What used to require scrolling through hundreds of rows now takes a few clicks.
kiosk-solution-templates: Introduced a 3-type product creation system: simple product, composite bundle (for promotional packages), and template. Templates let staff save any complete product configuration and reuse it - cutting product entry time for stores with recurring assortments.
kiosk-process-text: Worked remotely with Finenex (Korea) as 1 of 3 designers, together with a product owner, 5-6 developers, and 2 QA testers. Used Figma for design, Google Docs and Sheets for product specs and documentation. Manually tested flows on real devices - monoblocks and POS terminals. Conducted research on American and global market analogues to benchmark against best-in-class retail POS and inventory solutions.
